label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The passage starts by talking about Alexander Graham Bell inventing the telephone in 1876 and how it revolutionized communication. Then it shifts to how nowadays we use phones for more than just talking—like taking photos, internet, videos. Then it moves on to the development of mobile phones, the first mobile call in 1973 by Martin Cooper, their availability to the public, and how they became associated with a certain social group (yuppies). Then it discusses the mid-90s when mobiles became cheaper and more common, changing in design. The passage also mentions the impact of SMS on how we communicate, including changing timekeeping and language. It ends by reflecting on how far telephony has come, even mentioning text speak as an example. So the main focus here is the evolution of mobile phones. It starts with the traditional telephone, moves into mobile phones, their development over time, and the impact of features like SMS. The options are (A) Bell's invention, (B) SMS as a new way, (C) new functions of mobile phones, (D) development of the mobile phone. Option A is about Bell's invention, which is part of the passage but not the main focus. The main point is the development of mobile phones, which includes the invention but goes beyond it. Option B is SMS, which is discussed but again, as a part of the broader topic. Option C talks about new functions, which is covered (like using phones for photos, internet), but again, the passage is more about the development over time rather than just the functions.
